## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.

🟢 PR #36: feat(assistant): smoother HITL workflow and pre-merge release artefacts
  fcamblor
  OPEN | ✓
  Checks: 1/3 passed
  https://github.com/fcamblor/mac-ai-trackers/pull/36

  ## Summary
  
  Improvements to the `assistant-*` skill family driven by friction
  observed during the full `copilot-cli` onboarding run (issue #33).
  
  - **Mechanical execution after HITL decisions.** Every skill now proposes
    the `gh issue edit` / `gh pr review` / `gh issue close` commands itself
    with a single Y/n confirmation, instead of asking the maintainer to
    copy-paste. The decision still belongs to the maintainer; only the
    execution is automated.
  - **README and release notes move pre-merge.** `assistant-merge` now
    commits README + `docs/vendors/index.md` updates to the PR branch and
    embeds the release-notes draft in the squash-merge commit body.
    `assistant-release` is simplified to copy that body into the GitHub
    release once a tag ships.
  - **Shared handoff rule.** New `.claude/rules/skill-handoff.md` centralises
    the Phase-A self-check (mutex auto-correction), PR pre-flight
    (draft/mergeable/author-vs-reviewer), and HITL execution pattern.
    Every skill references it.
  - **New `assistant-propose` skill.** Classifies a free-form intent
    ("I'd like to support vendor X") and files the right issue with
    pre-filled fields, replacing the manual issue-form copy-paste path.
  - **Bootstrap labels check.** The router runs `gh label list` in Phase 0
    and proposes `scripts/bootstrap-onboarding-labels.sh` if missing.
  - **PR pre-flight in review and merge.** Detects draft state, merge
    conflicts, and author-vs-reviewer collision (forcing `--comment` over
    `--approve`).
  - **Doc-existence convergence.** `assistant-triage` Phase B enforces the
    same rule as `assistant-implement` Phase C, so the workflow no longer
    stalls on a discrepancy already known to the triage.
  - **Re-run cadence for `assistant-tester-followup`.** Documented the
    `/loop` wrapper for cross-comment polling.
  
  ## Test plan
  
  - [ ] Read `.claude/rules/skill-handoff.md` end-to-end and verify it
        matches the intent of the changes in each skill.
  - [ ] Walk a hypothetical `type:new-assistant` issue through the eight
        phases mentally; confirm no skill ends with an unsolicited
        copy-paste request.
  - [ ] Verify `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` §3.1.1, §3.1.6, §3.1.8 and §4
        stay self-consistent (no orphan reference to README updates in
        release phase, no missing reference to the new `assistant-propose`
        skill).
  - [ ] On the next real vendor change, validate that `assistant-merge`
        Phase C–E actually commits artefacts pre-merge and that
        `assistant-release` Phase B copies the merge commit body cleanly.
  
  🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

📄 .claude/rules/skill-handoff.md
  @@ -0,0 +1,67 @@
  +---
  +paths: .claude/skills/assistant*/SKILL.md
  +---
  +
  +# Skill handoff conventions
  +
  +Every `assistant-*` skill ends a phase by transitioning a GitHub label
  +and (often) posting a comment. To remove copy-paste friction from the
  +maintainer, follow these conventions uniformly.
  +
  +## 1. Phase-A self-check: detect label drift before doing anything
  +
  +At the start of every `assistant-*` skill (Phase A), after reading the
  +issue:
  +
  +- Detect a **mutex violation** (more than one `phase:*` label
  +  simultaneously). The CI workflow `phase-label-gate.yml` enforces the
  +  invariant, but the workflow may not have caught up yet (concurrency
  +  serialisation, GitHub event lag). When the local read sees more than
  +  one `phase:*` label, propose to remove the stale ones in a single
  +  `gh issue edit` and confirm Y/n before proceeding.
  +- Detect a **scope mismatch** (issue is not at the expected phase for
  +  this skill). Refuse explicitly and name the right skill instead of
  +  silently working anyway.
  +
  +## 2. HITL decisions vs mechanical execution
  +

---
paths: .claude/skills/assistant*/SKILL.md
---

# Skill handoff conventions

Every `assistant-*` skill ends a phase by transitioning a GitHub label
and (often) posting a comment. To remove copy-paste friction from the
maintainer, follow these conventions uniformly.

## 1. Phase-A self-check: detect label drift before doing anything

At the start of every `assistant-*` skill (Phase A), after reading the
issue:

- Detect a **mutex violation** (more than one `phase:*` label
  simultaneously). The CI workflow `phase-label-gate.yml` enforces the
  invariant, but the workflow may not have caught up yet (concurrency
  serialisation, GitHub event lag). When the local read sees more than
  one `phase:*` label, propose to remove the stale ones in a single
  `gh issue edit` and confirm Y/n before proceeding.
- Detect a **scope mismatch** (issue is not at the expected phase for
  this skill). Refuse explicitly and name the right skill instead of
  silently working anyway.

## 2. HITL decisions vs mechanical execution

The decision belongs to the maintainer (approve vs reject, merge vs
hold, count a tester vs ask for follow-up). The mechanical execution of
that decision — `gh issue edit --add-label`, `gh issue comment`,
`gh pr ready`, `gh release edit` — does NOT belong to the maintainer.

Pattern to follow at the end of each phase:

1. Surface the decision the maintainer must make.
2. Once they confirm the verdict (in plain text or by selecting an
   option), show the exact `gh` commands you intend to run.
3. Ask for a single Y/n confirmation covering the whole block.
4. Execute. Do not split into multiple confirmation rounds for what is
   logically one transition.

Never end a phase with "now please run `gh issue edit ...`" expecting
the maintainer to copy-paste. The skill is the operator; the maintainer
is the deciding party.

## 3. Per-skill PR pre-flight (review-and-onward skills)

Skills that touch a PR (`assistant-review`, `assistant-merge`) must
check, in Phase A, before doing any work:

- `gh pr view <pr> --json isDraft,mergeable,author,headRefOid`
- Refuse with a concrete instruction if `isDraft=true` (suggest
  `gh pr ready <pr>`) or `mergeable=CONFLICTING` (suggest a rebase).
- For `assistant-review`, when `author.login` equals the current
  `gh api user` login, use `gh pr review --comment` instead of
  `--approve` (GitHub forbids self-approval).

## 4. Phase-label transition table

When a skill needs to flip `phase:*`, the canonical one-liner is:

```sh
gh issue edit <n> --remove-label "phase:<from>" --add-label "phase:<to>"
```

Always include both `--remove-label` and `--add-label` so the
mutex-violation auto-revert in `phase-label-gate.yml` never fires.

2	name: assistant-merge
3	description: Verify gates one last time and squash-merge an assistant PR (new-assistant or vendor-evolution). Use when the issue is at phase:merge-ready.
4	model: sonnet
5	---
6	
7	# Assistant merge
8	
9	## Prerequisite
10	
11	Read `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`; it owns the threshold rules
12	(including the `kind:urgent-fix` escape hatch) and the phase
13	transitions. Read `.claude/rules/skill-handoff.md` for the shared
14	Phase-A self-check and PR pre-flight pattern. If this skill and the
15	spec disagree, the spec wins.
16	
17	## Argument
18	
19	A GitHub issue number.
20	
21	## Phases
22	
23	### Phase A — Verify scope, phase, and PR pre-flight
24	
25	Apply the Phase-A self-check from `.claude/rules/skill-handoff.md`
26	(mutex auto-correction). Refuse unless the issue is at
27	`phase:merge-ready`. Read the issue's `type:*` and (when present)
28	`kind:*` labels — they drive the threshold.
29	
30	Run the PR pre-flight (`gh pr view <pr> --json isDraft,mergeable`).
31	Refuse if `isDraft=true` (cannot merge a draft) or `mergeable` is not
32	`MERGEABLE` (resolve conflicts first).
33	
34	### Phase B — Re-verify gates
35	
36	Re-run the tester tally (same logic as `assistant-tester-followup`)
37	against the current state. Re-run the reviewer checklist. Refuse to
38	merge if any gate is not green.
39	
40	Threshold rules:
41	
42	- `type:vendor-evolution` + `kind:urgent-fix` → ≥ 1 confirmation is
43	  sufficient.
44	- All other cases → ≥ 2 distinct confirmations on the latest build
45	  SHA, with audited-clean attached logs.
46	
47	The PR author is allowed to count as one of those confirmations when
48	they post a sign-off comment as a tester.
49	
50	### Phase C — Pre-merge artefacts (commit on the PR branch)
51	
52	Before merging, push the user-facing updates onto the PR branch so they
53	ship in the same commit as the code:
54	
55	- `type:new-assistant` → update every README touchpoint that
56	  enumerates supported vendors (see `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`
57	  §3.1.6 for the full list — at minimum "Supported Assistants" and
58	  "Cache and Logs"; scan for any other Claude/Codex enumeration the
59	  README has grown since). Also add a row to `docs/vendors/index.md`
60	  if it exists. After editing, re-grep the README for the previously
61	  supported vendor slugs to confirm no enumeration was missed.
62	- `type:vendor-evolution` →
63	  - No README change (the vendor is already listed).
64	  - For `kind:breaking`, ensure the README's compatibility note (or the
65	    `Min app version` annotation in the vendor doc) is reflected
66	    wherever users decide which version to install.
67	
68	Show the diff to the user, then propose to commit & push:
69	
70	```sh
71	git checkout <pr-branch>
72	git add README.md docs/vendors/index.md
73	git commit -m "docs(<slug>): announce <Display Name> in README and vendor index"
74	# Re-grep the README to confirm no Claude/Codex enumeration was missed:
75	# `grep -nE 'Claude Code|Codex|claude-usages|codex-usages' README.md`
76	git push
77	```
78	
79	(Skip the commit entirely when no artefact change is needed —
80	`vendor-evolution` non-breaking.)
81	
82	### Phase D — Draft release notes for the merge commit body
83	
84	Draft the release-notes text now. It becomes the single source of
85	truth that the maintainer (or a release-engineering workflow) will
86	aggregate into the GitHub release notes when cutting the next tag.
87	Required content per type/kind, see `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`
88	§3.1.6:
89	
90	- All types — credit testers by handle (pull from the latest
91	  `assistant-tester-tally:sticky` comment).
92	- `kind:breaking` — prefix with
93	  `BREAKING: <vendor> connector requires <next-version>+` and explain

---
name: assistant-merge
description: Verify gates one last time and squash-merge an assistant PR (new-assistant or vendor-evolution). Use when the issue is at phase:merge-ready.
model: sonnet
---

# Assistant merge

## Prerequisite

Read `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`; it owns the threshold rules
(including the `kind:urgent-fix` escape hatch) and the phase
transitions. Read `.claude/rules/skill-handoff.md` for the shared
Phase-A self-check and PR pre-flight pattern. If this skill and the
spec disagree, the spec wins.

## Argument

A GitHub issue number.

## Phases

### Phase A — Verify scope, phase, and PR pre-flight

Apply the Phase-A self-check from `.claude/rules/skill-handoff.md`
(mutex auto-correction). Refuse unless the issue is at
`phase:merge-ready`. Read the issue's `type:*` and (when present)
`kind:*` labels — they drive the threshold.

Run the PR pre-flight (`gh pr view <pr> --json isDraft,mergeable`).
Refuse if `isDraft=true` (cannot merge a draft) or `mergeable` is not
`MERGEABLE` (resolve conflicts first).

### Phase B — Re-verify gates

Re-run the tester tally (same logic as `assistant-tester-followup`)
against the current state. Re-run the reviewer checklist. Refuse to
merge if any gate is not green.

Threshold rules:

- `type:vendor-evolution` + `kind:urgent-fix` → ≥ 1 confirmation is
  sufficient.
- All other cases → ≥ 2 distinct confirmations on the latest build
  SHA, with audited-clean attached logs.

The PR author is allowed to count as one of those confirmations when
they post a sign-off comment as a tester.

### Phase C — Pre-merge artefacts (commit on the PR branch)

Before merging, push the user-facing updates onto the PR branch so they
ship in the same commit as the code:

- `type:new-assistant` → update every README touchpoint that
  enumerates supported vendors (see `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`
  §3.1.6 for the full list — at minimum "Supported Assistants" and
  "Cache and Logs"; scan for any other Claude/Codex enumeration the
  README has grown since). Also add a row to `docs/vendors/index.md`
  if it exists. After editing, re-grep the README for the previously
  supported vendor slugs to confirm no enumeration was missed.
- `type:vendor-evolution` →
  - No README change (the vendor is already listed).
  - For `kind:breaking`, ensure the README's compatibility note (or the
    `Min app version` annotation in the vendor doc) is reflected
    wherever users decide which version to install.

Show the diff to the user, then propose to commit & push:

```sh
git checkout <pr-branch>
git add README.md docs/vendors/index.md
git commit -m "docs(<slug>): announce <Display Name> in README and vendor index"
# Re-grep the README to confirm no Claude/Codex enumeration was missed:
# `grep -nE 'Claude Code|Codex|claude-usages|codex-usages' README.md`
git push
```

(Skip the commit entirely when no artefact change is needed —
`vendor-evolution` non-breaking.)

### Phase D — Draft release notes for the merge commit body

Draft the release-notes text now. It becomes the single source of
truth that the maintainer (or a release-engineering workflow) will
aggregate into the GitHub release notes when cutting the next tag.
Required content per type/kind, see `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`
§3.1.6:

- All types — credit testers by handle (pull from the latest
  `assistant-tester-tally:sticky` comment).
- `kind:breaking` — prefix with
  `BREAKING: <vendor> connector requires <next-version>+` and explain
  what users on older versions will see (typically: `lastError`
  replacing live metrics).
- `kind:urgent-fix` — affected subset, since when, what the fix does.

Show the draft to the user for sign-off before Phase E.

### Phase E — Squash-merge

Use the standard commit convention from `docs/GIT-CONVENTIONS.md`. For
`kind:breaking`, ensure the commit title carries the `!` Conventional
Commits breaking marker. Embed the release-notes draft from Phase D as
the **body** of the squash-merge commit:

```sh
gh pr merge <pr> --squash \
  --subject "<conventional title>" \
  --body "<release-notes draft from Phase D>"
```

### Phase F — Apply `phase:merged`

```sh
gh issue edit <n> --remove-label "phase:merge-ready" --add-label "phase:merged"
```

Comment on the issue summarizing the merge and stating that the issue
will stay open until the next tagged release. For `kind:urgent-fix`,
also note: "Issue will stay at `phase:released` for follow-up tester
confirmations after the release ships."

### Phase G — Hand off

This is the terminal phase for the assistant skill family. The issue
stays open at `phase:merged` until a tagged release ships including the
merge commit; at that point the maintainer (or a release-engineering
workflow) transitions to `phase:released` and closes the issue.

The squash-merge commit body authored in Phase D is the single source
of truth for the release notes. It is the maintainer's responsibility
to aggregate merge commit bodies into the GitHub release notes when
they cut a tag — this skill does not edit `gh release` (it is a
destructive operation that would clobber concurrent vendor changes
shipped in the same release).

# Markdown authoring rules

## Language: English only

All Markdown files in this repository MUST be written in English, regardless of the conversation language. This includes `CLAUDE.md`, every file under `docs/`, `README.md`, and any new `*.md`.

- When creating a new Markdown file, write it in English from the start even if the user's prompt is in another language.
- When editing an existing Markdown file that contains non-English prose, translate the edited sections to English instead of preserving mixed languages.
- Code blocks, file paths, commands, and identifiers keep their original form — this rule applies to natural-language prose only.

## Write for longevity

Markdown in this repository is long-lived context. Optimize each file so it does not need to be rewritten as the code evolves. Avoid anything that ties documentation to a specific moment in time or to renamable artifacts:

- Do NOT reference specific commit SHAs, PR numbers, or branch names.
- Do NOT describe the project as being in a transient state ("currently a Hello World", "not yet implemented", "WIP"). If a feature does not exist, simply do not document it.
- Do NOT speculate about intent or future direction ("the name suggests…", "will eventually…"). Document what the code actually does today in terms that remain true tomorrow.
- Do NOT list every file, symbol, or dependency when a structural description is enough — exhaustive lists drift out of date quickly.
- Avoid naming specific files, types, or symbols that could be renamed or moved. Prefer describing roles and responsibilities ("the SwiftUI entry point", "the package manifest") over fixed identifiers. When a path is truly stable because a tool mandates it (e.g. `Package.swift` for Swift Package Manager, `.claude/rules/` for Claude Code), it is fine to use it.
- Do NOT duplicate information that already lives authoritatively in code or tool manifests (version numbers, platform minimums, target lists). Point readers at the source of truth instead of mirroring it.

When in doubt, ask: "Will this sentence still be accurate after the next three features ship, or after a plausible rename?" If not, rephrase or remove it.

## Single source of truth across Markdown files

When two Markdown files cover the same topic, exactly one is the authoritative spec; the other references it and adds only what is genuinely its own (workflow steps, scaffolding procedure, agent phases, etc.).

This applies in particular to these pairs:

- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (skill that implements it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/agents/<name>.md` (agent prompt that uses it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/commands/<name>.md` (slash command that triggers it)

Rules:

- Templates, file formats, naming conventions, and directory layouts belong in the spec, NOT in the operational artifact.
- The operational artifact MUST start with a one-line pointer ("Read `docs/<topic>.md` first; it defines X, Y, Z.") and MUST include a conflict-resolution line ("If this artifact disagrees with `docs/<topic>.md`, the spec wins.").
- Before adding concrete content (a template block, a format string, a list of sections) to an operational artifact, check whether that content already exists in a `docs/` spec. If it does, reference it instead of copying it.

## Exception: `roadmap/`

Files under `roadmap/` intentionally describe planned, not-yet-implemented work. The "Write for longevity" rules above (no transient state, no speculation about future direction) do NOT apply there — a roadmap is precisely a record of intent and ordering that will evolve as features ship. The English-only rule still applies. See `docs/ROADMAP.md` for the roadmap file conventions.

# Skill / agent / command authoring

Skills, agents, and slash commands are **operational** artifacts: they describe workflow (phases, steps, prompts). They are NOT the place to define domain conventions.

## Delegate specs to `docs/`

If the artifact implements a process that has (or deserves) a spec under `docs/`:

1. Write or extend the spec in `docs/<topic>.md` first. The spec owns: directory layout, naming conventions, file templates, list of required sections, line/format grammars.
2. In the artifact, include near the top:
   - A short "Prerequisite: read `docs/<topic>.md`" pointer, naming what the spec provides.
   - A conflict-resolution line: "If this artifact and `docs/<topic>.md` disagree, the spec wins; flag it to the user."
3. Keep the artifact focused on workflow only: phases (gather / validate / generate / confirm), questions to ask the user, duplicate checks, tool invocations.

## What NOT to copy into an artifact

- Concrete template blocks (```` ```markdown ... ``` ````) that repeat the spec's template.
- Explicit section lists that restate the spec's structure.
- Format strings (e.g. index line grammars, slug grammars) already fixed by the spec.

Reference them by pointing at the spec instead.

## When there is no spec

If the process is too small to deserve a `docs/` spec, keep everything inline in the artifact — but then no spec file should exist for that topic. Do not split a small topic across two files.

# Code Review — PR #36 : `feat(assistant): smoother HITL workflow and pre-merge release artefacts`

## Vue d'ensemble

Cette PR refactore la famille de skills `assistant-*` suite aux frictions observées pendant l'onboarding `copilot-cli` (issue #33). Trois axes majeurs :

1. **Mécanisation des décisions HITL** — les skills exécutent désormais les commandes `gh` après une confirmation Y/n unique, au lieu de demander au mainteneur de copier-coller.
2. **Déplacement des artefacts user-facing en pré-merge** — README et release notes sont posés sur la branche PR avant le squash-merge ; `assistant-release` est supprimé.
3. **Centralisation des conventions** — nouveau `.claude/rules/skill-handoff.md` (chargé via `paths:` frontmatter) qui factorise mutex auto-correction, PR pre-flight et pattern HITL.

S'ajoutent un nouveau skill `assistant-propose` (intention → issue), un Phase 0 bootstrap-labels sur le router, et la mise à jour README pour copilot-cli.

## Qualité et conventions

### Points forts

- **Cohérence spec ↔ skill** : `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` reste l'autorité ; chaque skill pointe dessus avec la clause "spec wins". Conforme à `.claude/rules/markdown-authoring.md`.
- **Factorisation propre** via `skill-handoff.md` avec `paths: .claude/skills/assistant*/SKILL.md` — bonne utilisation de la règle path-based introduite récemment (commit `1a2b271`).
- **Convergence Phase B triage ↔ Phase C implement** : la duplication assumée est documentée comme défense en profondeur, ce qui ferme un vrai gap.
- **Justification explicite du non-edit de `gh release`** dans `assistant-merge` Phase G : "destructive operation that would clobber concurrent vendor changes" — exactement le niveau de "WHY" qu'on attend.
- **README copilot-cli** suit la même structure que les sections Claude/Codex existantes.

### Suggestions / risques

1. **`.claude/rules/skill-handoff.md` §3 — formulation "Refuse" pour `assistant-merge`** : le rule dit "Refuse with a concrete instruction if `isDraft=true` (suggest `gh pr ready <pr>`)" mais pour `assistant-review`, draft n'est PAS un blocker (le skill peut review une draft, c'est juste un follow-up à signaler). Le `assistant-review/SKILL.md` Phase A le reflète correctement, mais le rule §3 mélange les deux cas dans la même phrase. Clarifier : "draft bloque le merge ; pour review c'est une note de hand-off."

2. **`assistant-merge` Phase C — commande `git commit` proposée** : `git add README.md docs/vendors/index.md` sera ajouté inconditionnellement. Pour `vendor-evolution` non-breaking, la note "Skip the commit entirely" est en bas mais la commande proposée juste avant ne discrimine pas. Un mainteneur pressé pourrait commiter un noop. Mieux : conditionner explicitement le bloc shell sous l'item `new-assistant`, et n'afficher rien pour vendor-evolution.

3. **`assistant-merge` Phase C — commentaire `# Re-grep…` dans le bloc commit** : un commentaire shell inline dans le snippet à exécuter est ambigu — soit on l'exécute (et alors mets-le AVANT le `git add`), soit c'est une note pour le mainteneur (et alors sors-le du bloc). Actuellement après `git commit` il est trop tard pour réagir au grep.

4. **`assistant-propose` Phase 0** : "Run the bootstrap check from `assistant` Phase 0" sans pointer une référence stable. Comme c'est un duplicat de logique, soit factoriser dans `skill-handoff.md` §X, soit citer le snippet `gh label list` directement (≤ 5 lignes, acceptable inline pour réduire l'indirection).

5. **`assistant/SKILL.md` Phase 0 — pipe shell** : `grep -E '…' | wc -l` puis compare à `< 3`. Avec `set -o pipefail` ou un grep qui ne match pas, on a un `wc -l` qui sort `0` — OK. Mais sans line-anchor strict, le `^(…)$` est correct. RAS, juste vérifier que le mainteneur lit bien la sortie attendue (le skill devrait afficher le count).

6. **`docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` §3.1.8** — phrase finale "User-facing README and `docs/vendors/index.md` updates are NOT part of this phase — they ship in the merge commit (see 3.1.6)." : excellent guard contre la régression vers l'ancien comportement. Garder.

7. **`assistant-tester-followup` "Re-run cadence"** : recommande `/loop /assistant-tester-followup <n>` — vérifier que `/loop` ne déclenche pas le Phase-A self-check + mutex check à chaque tick au point de générer du bruit GitHub. (Le check est read-only sauf violation, donc OK en pratique, mais ça mériterait une ligne "le self-check est idempotent et silencieux quand l'invariant tient".)

8. **Suppression d'`assistant-release`** : bien gérée — `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` retire toute mention, et le tableau §4 ne le liste plus. Vérifier qu'aucun autre skill (notamment hors `assistant-*`) ne le référence encore. (`grep -r assistant-release .claude/ docs/` recommandé en CI ou ici manuellement.)

9. **Frontmatter `paths:` dans `skill-handoff.md`** : `paths: .claude/skills/assistant*/SKILL.md` charge la règle pour tous les skills assistant — bien. Vérifier que l'`assistant-reverify` (qui n'est pas modifié dans cette PR) ne pâtit pas du chargement automatique (il n'utilise pas le pattern HITL au sens strict).

## Couverture de test

- Pas de test code (changes purement workflow / docs). Le test plan de la PR pose les bonnes questions (walk-through mental, vérification de cohérence inter-sections). 
- Suggestion : ajouter à la to-do un `grep -r 'assistant-release'` pour confirmer la suppression complète, et un `grep -nE 'Claude Code\|Codex\|Copilot' README.md` pour valider que copilot-cli a été ajouté partout (le commit `0ecbdb6` a déjà documenté tous les touchpoints).

## Sécurité

RAS. Les commandes `gh` proposées passent toujours par une confirmation Y/n, et le rule §2 interdit explicitement l'exécution silencieuse. Pas de credentials touchées.

## Verdict

**Approuvable** après les ajustements 2 et 3 (le bloc commit dans `assistant-merge` Phase C peut induire en erreur). Les autres points sont des nice-to-have.

# Assistant merge

## Prerequisite

Read `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`; it owns the threshold rules
(including the `kind:urgent-fix` escape hatch) and the phase
transitions. Read `.claude/rules/skill-handoff.md` for the shared
Phase-A self-check and PR pre-flight pattern. If this skill and the
spec disagree, the spec wins.

## Argument

A GitHub issue number.

## Phases

### Phase A — Verify scope, phase, and PR pre-flight

Apply the Phase-A self-check from `.claude/rules/skill-handoff.md`
(mutex auto-correction). Refuse unless the issue is at
`phase:merge-ready`. Read the issue's `type:*` and (when present)
`kind:*` labels — they drive the threshold.

Run the PR pre-flight (`gh pr view <pr> --json isDraft,mergeable`).
Refuse if `isDraft=true` (cannot merge a draft) or `mergeable` is not
`MERGEABLE` (resolve conflicts first).

### Phase B — Re-verify gates

Re-run the tester tally (same logic as `assistant-tester-followup`)
against the current state. Re-run the reviewer checklist. Refuse to
merge if any gate is not green.

Threshold rules:

- `type:vendor-evolution` + `kind:urgent-fix` → ≥ 1 confirmation is
  sufficient.
- All other cases → ≥ 2 distinct confirmations on the latest build
  SHA, with audited-clean attached logs.

The PR author is allowed to count as one of those confirmations when
they post a sign-off comment as a tester.

### Phase C — Pre-merge artefacts (commit on the PR branch)

Before merging, push the user-facing updates onto the PR branch so they
ship in the same commit as the code:

- `type:new-assistant` → update every README touchpoint that
  enumerates supported vendors (see `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`
  §3.1.6 for the full list — at minimum "Supported Assistants" and
  "Cache and Logs"; scan for any other Claude/Codex enumeration the
  README has grown since). Also add a row to `docs/vendors/index.md`
  if it exists. After editing, re-grep the README for the previously
  supported vendor slugs to confirm no enumeration was missed.
- `type:vendor-evolution` →
  - No README change (the vendor is already listed).
  - For `kind:breaking`, ensure the README's compatibility note (or the
    `Min app version` annotation in the vendor doc) is reflected
    wherever users decide which version to install.

Show the diff to the user, then propose to commit & push:

```sh
git checkout <pr-branch>
git add README.md docs/vendors/index.md
git commit -m "docs(<slug>): announce <Display Name> in README and vendor index"
# Re-grep the README to confirm no Claude/Codex enumeration was missed:
# `grep -nE 'Claude Code|Codex|claude-usages|codex-usages' README.md`
git push
```

(Skip the commit entirely when no artefact change is needed —
`vendor-evolution` non-breaking.)

### Phase D — Draft release notes for the merge commit body

Draft the release-notes text now. It becomes the single source of
truth that the maintainer (or a release-engineering workflow) will
aggregate into the GitHub release notes when cutting the next tag.
Required content per type/kind, see `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`
§3.1.6:

- All types — credit testers by handle (pull from the latest
  `assistant-tester-tally:sticky` comment).
- `kind:breaking` — prefix with
  `BREAKING: <vendor> connector requires <next-version>+` and explain
  what users on older versions will see (typically: `lastError`
  replacing live metrics).
- `kind:urgent-fix` — affected subset, since when, what the fix does.

Show the draft to the user for sign-off before Phase E.

### Phase E — Squash-merge

Use the standard commit convention from `docs/GIT-CONVENTIONS.md`. For
`kind:breaking`, ensure the commit title carries the `!` Conventional
Commits breaking marker. Embed the release-notes draft from Phase D as
the **body** of the squash-merge commit:

```sh
gh pr merge <pr> --squash \
  --subject "<conventional title>" \
  --body "<release-notes draft from Phase D>"
```

### Phase F — Apply `phase:merged`

```sh
gh issue edit <n> --remove-label "phase:merge-ready" --add-label "phase:merged"
```

Comment on the issue summarizing the merge and stating that the issue
will stay open until the next tagged release. For `kind:urgent-fix`,
also note: "Issue will stay at `phase:released` for follow-up tester
confirmations after the release ships."

### Phase G — Hand off

This is the terminal phase for the assistant skill family. The issue
stays open at `phase:merged` until a tagged release ships including the
merge commit; at that point the maintainer (or a release-engineering
workflow) transitions to `phase:released` and closes the issue.

The squash-merge commit body authored in Phase D is the single source
of truth for the release notes. It is the maintainer's responsibility
to aggregate merge commit bodies into the GitHub release notes when
they cut a tag — this skill does not edit `gh release` (it is a
destructive operation that would clobber concurrent vendor changes
shipped in the same release).

content.text:
# Smart commit

Tu dois committer les changements git en cours dans le repo courant en suivant les guidelines ci-dessous.

## Contexte utilisateur

Intent fourni par l'utilisateur (peut être vide) : ``

## Étapes

### 1. Analyser l'état du repo

Lance en parallèle :
- `git status` (sans `-uall`)
- `git diff --staged` et `git diff` pour voir les changements
- `git log -n 5 --pretty=format:'%h %s%n%b%n---'` pour voir les 4-5 derniers commits

### 2. Détecter les conventions du projet

À partir des 4-5 derniers commits :
- **Conventional commits ?** Si la majorité suit le format `type(scope): message` (feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, perf, build, ci...) → utilise ce format. Sinon → format libre.
- **Langue ?** Détecte la langue dominante des messages (français, anglais, autre) → utilise la même langue. En cas de doute, français.
- **Style ?** Impératif vs descriptif, majuscule initiale ou non, présence de scope, etc. → imite.

### 3. Détecter un éventuel fixup

Si l'intent utilisateur mentionne un correctif (mots-clés : "fix", "corrige", "répare", "bug", "issue", "problème", "régression", "broken"...) :

1. Identifie les fichiers modifiés (`git diff --name-only HEAD` + fichiers staged).
2. Pour chaque fichier, regarde `git log -n 10 --oneline -- <fichier>` pour voir quels commits récents l'ont touché.
3. Si **un seul commit récent** (dans les ~5 derniers, non encore pushé sur main/master partagé — vérifie avec `git log @{u}..HEAD` si upstream) est clairement à l'origine du code cassé, propose un **`git commit --fixup=<sha>`** au lieu d'un commit classique.
4. Si ambigu ou si le commit date d'avant `@{u}` (déjà pushé en amont partagé), reste sur un commit classique.

Ne fais **pas** de rebase autosquash automatiquement — laisse ce soin à l'utilisateur.

### 4. Rédiger le message

Règles impératives :
- **Le message explique le POURQUOI**, pas le QUOI. Le diff dit déjà ce qui change. Le message dit pourquoi ce changement existe (motivation, contrainte, problème résolu, contexte métier).
- Sujet court (≤72 caractères), en suivant la convention détectée.
- Si le pourquoi ne tient pas dans le sujet, ajoute un corps (ligne vide + explication). Sinon, pas de corps inutile.
- Pas de name-dropping de fichiers/fonctions dans le sujet — le diff suffit.
- Pas de phrases vides genre « Mise à jour du code » ou « Divers fixes ».

Si l'intent utilisateur est vide ou trop vague pour déduire le pourquoi, et que le diff ne le rend pas évident, **demande à l'utilisateur** avant de committer plutôt que d'inventer.

### 5. Staging et commit

- Si rien n'est staged, stage les fichiers pertinents **par nom** (jamais `git add -A` ni `git add .` pour éviter d'embarquer secrets/binaires).
- Ignore les fichiers suspects (`.env`, `credentials*`, clés privées, dumps volumineux). Préviens l'utilisateur si tu en vois.
- Crée le commit via HEREDOC pour préserver le formatage multiligne.
- **Ne jamais** utiliser `--no-verify`, `--amend` (sauf demande explicite), ni `--no-gpg-sign`.
- Si un hook pre-commit échoue : diagnostique la cause, corrige, re-stage, et crée un **nouveau** commit (pas d'amend).

### 6. Vérification finale

Après commit, lance `git status` et `git log -1` pour confirmer. Reporte brièvement : sha court + sujet + (fixup le cas échéant).

## Garde-fous

- Ne push jamais.
- Ne crée pas de commit vide.
- Ne touche pas à `git config`.
- Si plusieurs "unités logiques" distinctes sont mélangées dans le diff, préviens l'utilisateur et propose de splitter plutôt que de tout entasser. Tu peux utiliser `git stash` (avec `-u`/`--keep-index` si pertinent) pour isoler des sous-ensembles — mais cette commande n'est **pas** auto-approuvée, l'utilisateur devra confirmer à chaque usage : ne l'utilise que si c'est vraiment nécessaire.

- impeccable:distill: Strip designs to their essence by removing unnecessary complexity. Great design is simple, powerful, and clean. Use when the user asks to simplify, declutter, reduce noise, remove elements, or make a UI cleaner and more focused.
- impeccable:typeset: Improves typography by fixing font choices, hierarchy, sizing, weight, and readability so text feels intentional. Use when the user mentions fonts, type, readability, text hierarchy, sizing looks off, or wants more polished, intentional typography.
- impeccable:delight: Add moments of joy, personality, and unexpected touches that make interfaces memorable and enjoyable to use. Elevates functional to delightful. Use when the user asks to add polish, personality, animations, micro-interactions, delight, or make an interface feel fun or memorable.

- impeccable:harden: Make interfaces production-ready: error handling, empty states, onboarding flows, i18n, text overflow, and edge case management. - impeccable:clarify: Improve unclear UX copy, error messages, microcopy, labels, and instructions to make interfaces easier to understand. Use when the user mentions confusing text, unclear labels, bad error messages, hard-to-follow instructions, or wanting better UX writing.
- impeccable:quieter: Tones down visually aggressive or overstimulating designs, reducing intensity while preserving quality. Use when the user mentions too bold, too loud, overwhelming, aggressive, garish, or wants a calmer, more refined aesthetic.
- impeccable:adapt: Adapt designs to work across different screen sizes, devices, contexts, or platforms. Implements breakpoints, fluid layouts, and touch targets. Use when the user mentions responsive design, mobile layouts, breakpoints, viewport adaptation, or cross-device compatibility.

bc608e0 refactor(assistant): remove assistant-release skill — release is out of scope
The assistant skill family's terminal phase is phase:merged (PR squash-merged).
The release cadence is independent of vendor onboarding — a maintainer batches
multiple merged vendors and fixes into a single tag with their own stabilisation
window.

The assistant-release skill attempted to edit GitHub release notes via
`gh release edit <tag> --notes <body>`, which is a destructive full-overwrite.
This clobbers concurrent vendor changes shipped in the same release — if vendor A
merges first, then vendor B's release script erases A's notes.

The squash-merge commit body (authored by assistant-merge Phase D) is the single
source of truth. The maintainer aggregates these bodies when cutting a tag —
release-engineering work, decoupled from the skill family and out of scope here.

Changes:
- Remove .claude/skills/assistant-release/ directory.
- Mark phase:merged as terminal for the skill family in router and docs.
- Update assistant-merge Phase G to clarify the skill stops after the merge.
- Rewrite docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md §3.1.7 and §3.1.8 to describe the split.
- Update docs/REVIEW-CHECKLIST-ASSISTANT-CHANGE.md: pre-merge artefacts (README,
  release notes in commit body) are now verified by assistant-merge, not by a
  post-release step.

The phase:released label remains in the bootstrap script — it may be used by a
future CI workflow, but no assistant skill creates it.

authoring an assistant-* skill, defeating its purpose as the shared
execution pattern. Scope it to the assistant-* skills directory like the
other rules.

Entire-Checkpoint: e5c5894b461e

9624949 feat(.claude/skills/assistant-merge,release): move user-facing artefacts and release notes to pre-merge
Restructure the merge and release workflow to front-load user-facing updates.

In assistant-merge:
- New Phase C commits README and docs/vendors/index.md updates to the PR
  branch (new-assistant only; vendor-evolution has no README change).
- New Phase D drafts the release-notes text (testers credited, BREAKING
  context, urgent-fix context) for user sign-off.
- New Phase E embeds the release-notes draft in the squash-merge commit
  **body** (not the title), making it the single source of truth.
- Renumber subsequent phases (F, G).

In assistant-release:
- Simplify to Phase B: copy the squash-merge commit body into
  gh release edit (no regeneration of README or notes).
- Remove Phase B's artefact rewrites and Phase C's release-notes generation.
- All README and notes updates now ship in the merge commit.

This eliminates the post-tag editing friction and ensures README updates
land in the same commit as the code (rather than days later when the tag
exists).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: 03f8c33d5127

content.thinking:
L'état du repo montre :
- Branche actuelle : `new-vendor-assistant-improvements` (tracking origin/new-vendor-assistant-improvements)
- 1 fichier modifié (unstaged) : `.claude/skills/assistant-merge/SKILL.md`
- Pas de fichiers staged
- Les derniers commits sont en anglais avec le format conventional (docs, refactor, feat, fix)

L'intent utilisateur est vide, je dois donc analyser le diff pour comprendre le pourquoi du changement.

Le diff dans `.claude/skills/assistant-merge/SKILL.md` montre :
- Restructuration de la Phase C pour clarifier et diviser les cas (type:new-assistant vs type:vendor-evolution)
- Déplacement du grep de vérification AVANT le commit (plutôt que dans un commentaire shell après)
- Traitement séparé pour vendor-evolution non-breaking (skip le commit) vs breaking (commit optionnel)

C'est clairement une clarification / amélioration suite à la review que j'ai faite plus tôt. Je dois explicitement demander le contexte à l'utilisateur avant de procéder, puisque le diff ne parle que du "pourquoi" qu'implicitement (on sait qu'on répond aux points 2 et 3 de la review, mais c'est pas flagrant pour quelqu'un qui n'a pas lu la review).

En vérité, le contexte est clair : l'utilisateur a dit "fix les points 2 et 3" et j'ai modifié le fichier. Donc le pourquoi c'est : "Clarify Phase C conditions and move grep verification before commit to prevent noop commits and allow user inspection."

Mais laisse-moi checker si c'est déjà staged...


Le fichier n'est pas staged. Je dois le stage manuellement. Les conventions détectées indiquent du conventional commit en anglais avec scope. Ce changement est une clarification documentaire, donc je vais utiliser `docs(assistant-merge):` plutôt que `refactor` puisqu'on améliore la documentation du skill.

Le message de commit sera `docs(assistant-merge): clarify Phase C branching and move grep before commit`. Le corps expliquera pourquoi on rend la condition explicite pour new-assistant vs vendor-evolution afin d'éviter les commits vides, et pourquoi on place grep avant git add pour que le mainteneur puisse inspecter et corriger avant de stage. Je vais stage et commit en une seule action.

git add .claude/skills/assistant-merge/SKILL.md && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
docs(assistant-merge): clarify Phase C branching and move grep before commit

Restructure Phase C to explicitly condition the commit block on type:* label:
- new-assistant: mandatory commit with README and index updates.
- vendor-evolution non-breaking: skip commit entirely (no artefacts).
- vendor-evolution breaking: conditional commit only if edits needed.

Move the grep verification _before_ git add so the maintainer can inspect
the output and fix any missed enumeration before staging, preventing noop
commits. Surface an explicit instruction after grep to route to the next
step.

This addresses review feedback points 2 & 3: unambiguous branching path
and actionable verification loop.
EOF
)" && git status && git log -1 --pretty=format:'%h %s'
